title: Ink on Leather
timeEstimate: Pro-lane; minimal home attempt only on finished leather
stainChemistry: Ink chemistry varies by type (ballpoint / gel / Sharpie) — see ink-cotton chemistry detail. On leather, the substrate matters more than the ink: (1) FINISHED / pigmented leather has a polymer topcoat — ink may sit on the surface, sometimes responding to mild solvent + leather conditioner. (2) ANILINE / unfinished leather absorbs ink directly into the dyed hide — pro-only from minute one. The CRITICAL leather rules: NO acetone (destroys finish on contact), NO undiluted alcohol (strips finish), NO hydrogen peroxide (strips dye + finish + oils), NO chlorine bleach (destroys hide). Pro uses VDS or amyl acetate at controlled concentration + immediate leather conditioning. Home tool that lifts ink from cotton (90% IPA) damages leather finish.
whyThisWorks: On finished leather, a brief gentle approach (mild soap + leather-safe solvent applied carefully + immediate conditioning) can lift FRESH surface ink — sometimes. On aniline leather, even a fresh attempt risks dye stripping. Pro chemistry is leather-tuned (controlled VDS/amyl, leather conditioner) and home cannot match. The honest home protocol: identify finish, attempt minimal mild action only on finished leather, escalate fast.
defaultAssumption: Finished/pigmented leather assumed; aniline = pro from minute one. Most furniture / handbag / shoe leather is finished. Always condition after any wet treatment.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Water-drop test: beads = finished, soaks in = aniline (pro from minute one). Identify ink: ballpoint / gel / Sharpie — each takes different pro solvent.
Press white cloth on wet ink. Lift straight up, fresh cloth, repeat. No rubbing — pushes ink past topcoat.

spottingProtocol.equipment: VDS / amyl acetate per ink type, white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pro applies controlled solvent to white cloth, tamps lightly on the stain. Brief contact. Hidden-area test before attempt.
Mandatory after any wet treatment. Cleaning strips natural oils — un-conditioned leather cracks.

steps: 1. Identify leather (water-drop test) + ink type. Aniline = pro-only.
3. Hidden-area solvent test (VDS / amyl acetate per ink type).
warnings: NEVER acetone on leather — destroys finish on contact
NEVER hydrogen peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine bleach — strip dye + oils
Aniline leather = pro from minute one

homeSolutions.agent: Identify Your Leather First — Water-Drop Test
homeSolutions.instruction: Drop ONE bead of water on a hidden leather area. If it BEADS on top = finished/pigmented (proceed cautiously below). If it SOAKS IN immediately = aniline (STOP — pro from minute one). Aniline absorbs ink directly into the dyed hide; even fresh attempts risk stripping leather dye along with the ink. Don't gamble on aniline.

homeSolutions.agent: What NOT To Do — The Bad-Advice List for Ink
homeSolutions.instruction: The internet's worst leather-ink advice: hairspray (Aqua Net contains alcohol that strips finish), acetone, undiluted rubbing alcohol, peroxide, OxiClean, baking soda paste, magic eraser. ALL of these damage leather worse than ink. Magic eraser is a melamine sponge — abrasive — strips leather finish on contact. Hairspray is the most common bad recommendation; the alcohol content damages leather.

homeSolutions.agent: If Wet — Blot Immediately, No Rubbing
homeSolutions.instruction: Press a clean white cloth straight down on the wet ink. Lift straight up, press a fresh part of the cloth, repeat. Do NOT rub or wipe — rubbing pushes ink past wear spots in the topcoat into the dyed layer underneath. Get as much wet ink off the surface as possible before any chemistry.

homeSolutions.agent: Mild Soap on a BARELY-Damp Cloth — One Pass
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1–2 drops of Dawn (just two drops) in a cup of warm water. Dampen a clean white cloth, WRING IT OUT until barely damp. Wipe the stain area in gentle circles, OUTSIDE-IN. Then wipe with a plain-water-damp cloth to clear soap. Leather should never look visibly wet. ONE PASS — repeat strips finish.
Apply leather conditioner (Lexol or Bickmore) with a soft cloth per the label. Cleaning strips leather's natural oils; conditioning is non-negotiable to prevent cracking.

If a shadow remains after one careful pass, take it to a leather care specialist (NOT a regular dry cleaner). Tell them: "Ink on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, type [ballpoint / gel / Sharpie], age [hours / days], one home pass with mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner. No oxidizer, no acetone, no alcohol, no hairspray applied." Pros use VDS or amyl acetate at controlled concentration + leather conditioning — ink-type-specific chemistry home can't safely deploy.

steps: 1. Identify leather: water-drop test. Aniline = pro from minute one.
2. NO acetone, NO alcohol, NO peroxide, NO hairspray, NO magic eraser.
3. Press-blot wet ink immediately, no rubbing.
4. Mild soap (1–2 drops Dawn) on a BARELY-damp cloth, outside-in.
5. Plain-water damp wipe to clear soap.
7. Residual = pro leather specialist with ink type identified.
products: Mild dish soap (Dawn — 1–2 drops, no more)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if leather is aniline / unfinished, if a stain remains after one careful pass, or if anyone has applied harsh chemistry previously.
warnings: NEVER acetone — destroys leather on contact
NEVER hydrogen peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine — strip dye + oils
NEVER magic eraser — abrasive, strips finish
NEVER skip conditioner after wet cleaning

fiberSensitivities: Finished / pigmented leather: tolerates mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner
Aniline / unfinished: pro-only — even water rings

materialWarnings: Hairspray and magic eraser are the worst bad-advice items

escalation.when: Aniline / unfinished leather (always), shadow remaining after one pass, or anyone has applied harsh chemistry previously.
escalation.whatToTell: "Ink on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, type [ballpoint / gel / Sharpie], age [hours / days], one home pass with mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner. No oxidizer, no acetone, no alcohol, no hairspray, no magic eraser used."
escalation.specialistType: leather care specialist (NOT a regular dry cleaner — ask about VDS / amyl acetate spotting on leather)

contextQuestions: Water-drop test: bead, soak, or in-between?
Ink type — ballpoint / gel pen / Sharpie?
Furniture / handbag / jacket / shoes / car seat?
Has anyone applied hairspray, acetone, alcohol, peroxide, or magic eraser?
Color — light leather shows residual ink more
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Aniline leather is the unforgiving variant — common on high-end handbags + furniture. Sharpie on aniline is essentially permanent. Hairspray pre-applied = leather likely already stripped where the alcohol hit. Magic eraser pre-applied = finish abraded; pro will see fading where you used it.
